name: Mean Deviation — Average Distance from the Middle
whatIsIt: MEAN DEVIATION measures spread as the AVERAGE DISTANCE of the values from a centre (usually the mean). Because plain deviations (x − mean) always add up to ZERO, we take their ABSOLUTE values first. Mean deviation about the mean = Σ|x − mean| ÷ n. It is simpler than variance and tells you, on average, how far the data sits from the middle.
whyNeeded: A single average hides how spread out data is. Mean deviation gives a plain-English measure of spread — useful for comparing consistency of scores, temperatures or prices — and is a Class 11 NCERT statistics topic that appears in JEE and board exams.
realLifeUses: How consistent a batsman's scores are around his average
How much daily temperatures vary from the monthly mean
How steady a shop's daily sales are
Comparing which of two machines gives more uniform output

vocabulary.meaning: The difference x − mean for a value x.
Average of the absolute deviations: Σ|x − mean| ÷ n.

story.title: Whose Scores Are Steadier?
story.text: Aditi's cricket scores are 1, 3, 5, 7 with a mean of 4. 'How spread out are they?' asks Coach. Aditi finds each distance from 4: 3, 1, 1, 3 — she adds them (8) and divides by 4, getting 2. 'So on average my scores are 2 runs from my mean.' Coach nods: 'That's the mean deviation. And note — if you didn't take absolute values, the pluses and minuses would cancel to zero.'
story.extractedProblem: For scores 1, 3, 5, 7 with mean 4, what is the mean deviation about the mean?

visual.caption: Absolute distances from the mean (4) for 1, 3, 5, 7 are 3, 1, 1, 3. Their average, (3+1+1+3)/4 = 2, is the mean deviation.

teachingGallery.title: Distances, then average
teachingGallery.note: Absolute values first — signed ones cancel.

standardMethod.summary: Find the mean. Take each value's distance from the mean using absolute values. Add the distances and divide by n.
steps: Compute the mean of the data.
For each value, find |x − mean| (the distance, always positive).
Divide by the number of values n: mean deviation = Σ|x − mean| ÷ n.

workedExamples.problem: Find the mean deviation about the mean for 1, 3, 5, 7.
Why must we use absolute values? What does Σ(x − mean) always equal?
steps: Deviations above and below the mean cancel.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the signed deviations (x − mean) instead of their absolute values.
commonMistakes.fix: Signed deviations always cancel to 0. Use ABSOLUTE deviations |x − mean| for mean deviation.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the absolute deviations but forgetting to divide by n.
commonMistakes.fix: Mean deviation = (sum of |x − mean|) ÷ n — it is an AVERAGE distance.

easy.q: Mean deviation uses which of the deviations?

medium.q: The sum of signed deviations (x − mean) always equals?

questions.q: Sum of the signed deviations from the mean equals?
Mean deviation about the mean is…

teachBackPrompt: Take four of your test or game scores. Find the mean, then each score's distance from it, add the distances and divide by 4. Explain to a grown-up why you use absolute values (otherwise the pluses and minuses cancel to zero).

revisionCard.summary: Mean deviation = average distance from the centre = Σ|x − mean| ÷ n. Use ABSOLUTE deviations because signed deviations always sum to 0. It's a simple measure of spread (smaller = more consistent data).

rememberIt.hook: Mean deviation = Σ|x − mean| ÷ n (absolute distances)
rememberIt.unpack: Signed deviations sum to 0, so use |…|. Then average by dividing by n.

studentQuestions.a: Both measure spread. Mean deviation uses absolute distances (simpler); standard deviation squares them (used more in advanced statistics).

studentQuestions.q: Can mean deviation be taken about the median?
studentQuestions.a: Yes — mean deviation about the median exists too, and it is actually the smallest possible mean deviation for a data set.
